coredata.dc:title: Eggshell membrane-mimicking multifunctional nanofiber for in-situ skin wound healing
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Xiaofang
Chen, Yaqin
Fu, Bofei
Li, Kunjie
Huang, Donghong
Zheng, Chaohui
Liu, Minghuan
Yang, Da-Peng
coredata.dc:description: Eggshell membrane is a naturally-occurring protective barrier layer for chickens' incubation and shows the close similarity with extracellular matrix. To fully explore and utilize its' structure and active components via a mimicking way will be of great interest for wounds healing. Herein, the well-dispersed CuS nanoparticles were prepared by using eggshell membranes as templates with strong near-infrared absorption and photothermal properties. Furthermore, the as-prepared solution was combined with polyvinyl pyrrolidone and chitosan-derived fluorescent carbon dots for the mimetic synthesis of multifunctional nanofibrous membrane by a hand-held electrospinning device, which has the merits of in-situ operation, the extracellular matrix (ECM)-like architecture, hemostatic, radical scavenging, antibacterial, as well as accelerated healing of skin injury, etc. The electrospun-nanofiber membrane with optimal addition of 100 mg/L CuS nanoparticles was confirmed to be noncytotoxic on human fibroblasts and showed strong antibacterial activities against S. aureus and E. coli under NIR irradiation (980 nm). In addition, the radical scavenging ability was also proved by DPPH experiments. The animal experiments revealed that the nanofiber membrane could accelerate the wound healing process. The work lays down a simple and environmentally-friendly approach for the fabrication and development of promising wound healing materials in skin tissue engineering applications.

TEM images of (A) ESM/CuS NPs freshly prepared and (B) the one placed for more than one day at room temperature, revealing a dynamic assembling process. Inset is the histogram of particle size of ESM/CuS NPs.
(A) Bacterial growth after incubation in broth medium accompanied with (5 min) and without NIR irradiation; histogram of the antibacterial efficiency of ESM/CuS NPs to E. coli (B) and S. aureus (C).

head.ce:title: Eggshell membrane-mimicking multifunctional nanofiber for in-situ skin wound healing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Eggshell membrane is a naturally-occurring protective barrier layer for chickens' incubation and shows the close similarity with extracellular matrix. To fully explore and utilize its' structure and active components via a mimicking way will be of great interest for wounds healing. Herein, the well-dispersed CuS nanoparticles were prepared by using eggshell membranes as templates with strong near-infrared absorption and photothermal properties. Furthermore, the as-prepared solution was combined with polyvinyl pyrrolidone and chitosan-derived fluorescent carbon dots for the mimetic synthesis of multifunctional nanofibrous membrane by a hand-held electrospinning device, which has the merits of in-situ operation, the extracellular matrix (ECM)-like architecture, hemostatic, radical scavenging, antibacterial, as well as accelerated healing of skin injury, etc. The electrospun-nanofiber membrane with optimal addition of 100 mg/L CuS nanoparticles was confirmed to be noncytotoxic on human fibroblasts and showed strong antibacterial activities against S. aureus and E. coli under NIR irradiation (980 nm). In addition, the radical scavenging ability was also proved by DPPH experiments. The animal experiments revealed that the nanofiber membrane could accelerate the wound healing process. The work lays down a simple and environmentally-friendly approach for the fabrication and development of promising wound healing materials in skin tissue engineering applications.

ce:section.ce:para: The skin is a natural spacesuit of the human body, which will face up different degrees of harm due to burns, surgical operation, as well as mechanical injuries [1]. When it is damaged, an instant antibleeding action is needed for the exposed wounds. The wounds will soon become a favorable location for microbial colonization due to mismanagement. The microorganisms infecting wounds mainly come from the air, including Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) [2], etc. To fight for them, a popular way is to use antibiotics, which has caused great concern due to the emergence of drug-resistant bacteria. Therefore, finding an alternative technology or material to treat multidrug-resistant bacteria infected wounds has always been a hot topic in medical research. Besides meeting the requirements of antibacterial and hemostasis, an ideal wound healing material also needs to inhibit the excessive growth of free radicals in the wound tissue. Therefore, it is important to develop a simple and rapid method or material to effectively treat wounds, particularly, in the case of outdoors injuries caused by crash. The miscellaneous requirements for wounds healing have resulted in the flourish of biomaterials, such as, collagen, chitosan, silk, as well as gelatin [3]. However, it is still short of the multifunctional material to satisfy the requirements of wound repair by the intact combination of ECM-mimicking, hemostasis, antibacterial, as well as radicals-scanning. In addition, low-price and simple-preparation approach should also be considered for fabricating the wound dressings.

Eggshell membrane (abbreviated as ESM) plays an important role in maintaining the safety and quality of chicken during hatch. It is estimated that over 1012 eggshells are produced in the world, thereby leading to the occurrence of lots of ESM, which are often regarded as useless wastes. In fact, ESM is called as “phenix clothes” in traditional Chinese medicine and is used to cure the canker, stomach, etc. In Japan, it is a useful dressing of wounds for home care until now. With an in-depth understanding of the structure and biologically active ingredients of ESM by using modern advanced instruments, researchers have found that it is a multi-biomolecules complex in component, which is made up of collagen (Types I, V, X), glycoprotein, sulfur-rich proteins, keratin as well as hyaluronic acid [4,5]. Structurally, it is an interwoven fiber network structure with many pores of about 0.1–7 μm in diameter, which is beneficial to gas exchange and against bacterial invasion [6]. Importantly, it shares the similarities with the ECM in structure, thus ESM is widely used as a potential natural biomaterial or template [4,7], especially for tissue engineering [8]. For example, Pedersen et al. showed that the processed ESM powder could regulate cellular functions and increase the activity of matrix metalloproteinase type 2 (MMP-2), which was important in early wound healing process [9]. Luo et al. designed the eggshell membrane/hydroxyapatite (ESM-HA) composites with enhanced hydrophilicity and osteogenic activity by using a biomimetic mineralization technique, which could become a potential new material for future bone tissue repair [10]. Dhara et al. modified the ESM with electrospun chitosan/polycaprolactone nanofibers and fabricated a bilayer scaffold as a potential dermal substitute for wound healing application [11]. Han et al. revealed that carboxylic acid-treated eggshell membrane significantly promoted human dermal fibroblasts adhesion, viability, proliferation, and cytokine secretion [12]. Although the ESM as a bioactive material displayed excellent cell adhesion and proliferation ability, the limited size and insolubility hinder its further utilization. Therefore, reshaping the ESM into a tailored biomaterial to enhance its application value in skin wound healing is strongly required. We summed up the main research progress of ESM in recent decades (Table S1).

Electrospinning is a technique to produce ultrafine nanofibers by charging and ejecting a polymer melt or solution through a spinneret under a high-voltage electric field and to solidify or coagulate it to form a filament. The as-prepared nanofibers have been widely used in the biomedical field due to their excellent porosity and air permeability, especially in wound dressings [13–15]. Traditional electrospinning nanofibers are usually deposited on aluminum foil in vitro, then, they are cut and applied to the wound as needed, which is analogous to the use of traditional bandages with poor tightness on rough surfaces [16–18]. With the development of hand-held electrospinning device, nanofibers can be deposited on the wound in situ, which not only has a rapid hemostasis effect, but also avoids the direct contact with the wound, thereby reduces the patient's pain and avoids the risk of secondary injury [16,19]. Although the hand-held electrospinning device has lots of advantages, the healing effect largely depends on the quality of spinning solution. Considering the special requirements of electrospinning materials in medicine, choosing natural polymers (such as alginate, cellulose, silk fibroin, collagen, etc.) as electrospinning solution is becoming a hot topic in this field. However, these natural polymers usually lack the required functions such as antibacterial and radicals-scavenging abilities, which are extremely important in practical applications [20]. Adding antibacterial nanomaterials (Ag, ZnO, Cu etc. nanoparticles) or natural antioxidants (quercetin, curcumin, tannin) into spinning polymers solution is a prevalent strategy to obtain these functional nanofibers [21]. Due to the existence of many disulfide bonds in ESM, ESM is difficult to dissolve in water, which makes it inconvenient to handle in the form of spinning solution. Therefore, it is not conducive to becoming a biologically active nano-scaffold material in skin tissue engineering. However, researchers realized that ESM could serve as a biocompatible template to confine and regulate the nucleation and growth of metal sulfide NPs by utilizing the inert disulfide bond method of activated proteins by metal ions under alkaline conditions [22].

Copper sulfide (CuS), one of the most important p-type semiconductors in transition metal chalcogenides, has received extensive attention due to its unique plasmonic behavior [23,24]. As a low-price photothermal nanomaterial, it can replace the expensive Au analogs [25]. As well-known, photothermal therapy (PTT) has the advantages of high selectivity and minimal invasiveness, which is widely explored in the field of nanomedicine [26–28]. The mechanism is to convert the near-infrared (NIR) light energy of photothermal agents into heat so that some targets such as cancer cells or bacterial can be ablated through photothermal effects [29]. Unfortunately, the poor biocompatibility and ambiguous toxicity of CuS NPs limit their applications in medicine [30]. Moreover, due to the high aggregation effect, the photogenerated charge transfer rate on the surface of CuS is reduced, which also significantly hampers its performance [31]. An effective strategy is to choose a suitable protein as carrier to disperse and coat the surfaces of CuS NPs, which can overcome these shortcomings. For example, Liu et al. utilized egg white as template to prepare egg-encapsulated CuS NPs, which can be well dispersed and deposited directly on the skin without damaging the wound [16]. Our group had used the bovine serum albumin (BSA) as template to obtain the BSA-stabilized CuS (BSA-CuS) nanoparticles with the advantages of low toxicity, strong-NIR absorbing, and good biocompatibility, which is an efficient photothermal ablation agent of bacteria [32]. Theoretically, ESM has similar structure and components with egg white or BSA, it can be used as template to prepare CuS NPs with low price, excellent biocompatibility and strong NIR-absorbing function. Such-prepared bio-nano material can act as a cost-effective electrospinning solution to prepare bioactive nanofiber with strong antibacterial activities for skin wound healing.

Follow the requirements as above-mentioned, radicals-scanning ability is another important procedure during the wound healing process. Adding antioxidants into the nanofiber is a universal method to overcome the problem. With the flourishing research of fluorescent CDs, some interesting properties such as pH-sensing, UV-light screening, antibacterial and antioxidant are being actively explored and reported. Furthermore, to obtain these functional CDs with low toxicity and good biocompatibility, some “green” precursors such as tree leaves, glucose, vitamin C, etc. are widely adopted. Among them, chitosan is a naturally-occurring polysaccharide with nontoxic and biodegradable features, which can be regarded as an ideal carbon source for the green synthesis of CDs. To the best of our knowledge, there are few reports regarding the synthesis of fluorescent CDs by using chitosan as unique carbon source.

Herein, we use chitosan as green precursor to prepare fluorescent CDs via a hydrothermal reaction. The as-prepared CDs shows bright luminescent, antibacterial, as well as antioxidant activities. Furthermore, the functional CDs are mixed with NIR-absorbing CuS NPs solution, which was prepared by using eggshell membrane proteins as template and dispersing agent. Then, the mixed solution is electrospun in situ by using a handheld electrospinning device to form nanofibers with multifunctional features of hemostatic, radical scavenging, antibacterial, as well as NIR-absorbing. Last, the eggshell membrane-mimicking nanofiber is used for rat wound model with MRSA infection, which showed a satisfying result. This in-situ electrospinning technique combined with the functional solution (ESM, CDs, CuS NPs) can provide good habitats for cell migration and proliferation, avoid the direct contact with the wound, greatly reduce the pain, and have better density on the rough wound surface than bandages and gauze, which can accelerate wound healing process.

Copper nitrate trihydrate (Cu(NO3)2·3H2O), thioacetamide (CH3CSNH2), Chitosan, Citric acid monohydrate, hydrochloric acid, and nitric acid (HNO3) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). 2,2′-Diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl radical (DPPH·,96%) and 2,20-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) diammonium salt (ABTS, 98%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was purchased from Xilong Scientific Co., Ltd. Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, Mw = 1,300,000) was obtained from Tianjin Bodi Chemical Co., Ltd. All chemical reagents were analytical grade and used without further purification. Deionized water was obtained from a Milli-Q system. Bacteria were supplied by the department of clinical laboratory, the second affiliated hospital of Fujian Medical University. Nutrient agar and nutrient broth were obtained from Shanghai bowei biotechnology Co., Ltd. Waste eggshell was collected from the local canteen (Quanzhou Normal University) and carefully cleaned with water to remove the remained albumen. After that, they were soaked in deionized water for 1 h and stripped from eggshell. The detached eggshell membrane was rinsed several times with deionized water and subsequently soaked in hydrochloric acid (10%) for about 12 h to remove the mineral matters. After that, the ESM was thoroughly washed with deionized water for three times and dried at 60 °C in an oven. Finally, the dried ESM was crushed into powder for later use.

Typically, 1 g dried ESM powder was soaked in 50 mL NaOH solution (1 M) and stirred at 60 °C for about 5 h to get homogeneous solution. The dissolved ESM solution was filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane, and the resulting clear solution was used for the following experiment. Firstly, 1 mL Cu(NO3)2·3H2O (0.25 M) aqueous solution was added into 10 mL ESM solution and stirred for 30 min. Then, the pH of the mixture was adjusted to 6.8 with the addition of nitric acid, followed by adding 1 mL of CH3CSNH2 solution (0.12 M) slowly. After that, the homogeneous solution was bathed at 90 °C and kept stirring for 1 h. When the solution was found to become the dark green, it indicated the successful synthesis of ESM/CuS nanocomposites.

CDs were synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal carbonization method [33]. Citric acid monohydrate solution (0.5 M, 30 mL) and chitosan (1 g) was mixed evenly in a baker. Subsequently, 1 g of urea was added and the mixed solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon container and placed in an autoclave crucible for heating at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling at room temperature, a dark yellow solution was formed, which was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min and passed a 0.2 μm syringe filer. The solution was further dialyzed against ultrapure water using an osmosis membrane (molecular weight cutoff = 1 kDa) for 48 h to remove impurities. Then, the solution was dried at 80 °C on a hot plate and the fluorescence powder (CDs) was obtained for further characterization and use. The relevant fluorescence properties of as-prepared CDs were measured subsequently and exhibited an excitation-dependent emission behavior by changing the emission from 420–520 nm upon 320–460 nm excitations that were comparable to other studies on CDs [34,35].

In order to test the photothermal properties of ESM/CuS nanocomposites, 2 mL ESM/CuS solution with different Cu ions concentrations (37.5, 75, 150, 300, 600, 1200 mg/L) was respectively exposed to a 980 nm laser (MW-GX-980/3000 Mw, Jilin, China) at a power density of 2.5 W/cm2 for 10 min. And, a thermometer (Beckmann, China) was inserted into the solution to measure the temperature at different time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal ablation against S. aureus and E. coli
ce:section.ce:para:
The anti-bactericidal property of ESM/CuS nanocomposites against Gram-negative E. coli (ATCC 25922) and Gram-positive S. aureus (ATCC 25923) was tested by a simple colony counting method. The photothermal ablation experiment was carried out after culturing them in broth medium. The concentration of each microorganism was adjusted to 1 × 107 CFU/mL. Briefly, 10 mg ESM/CuS NPs was added to 500 μL of bacteria solution and mixed well, and in order to verify whether the material has the function of photothermal sterilization, we set up the NIR laser irradiation group (2.5 W/cm2, 5 min) and non-laser irradiation group. In contrast, LB agar with bacteria but no samples were performed using a similar experimental procedure. The percentage of antibacterial activity of the ESM/CuS NPs was calculated using the following formula:
Antibacterial efficiency
%
=
A − B
A
× 100
A and B represent the number of bacterial colonies cultured with and without antibacterial nanomaterials, respectively.

To further verify the antibacterial performance and photothermal sterilization performance of the ESM/CuS NPs, sterilization experiments were carried out with different concentrations of materials. Specifically, 100 μL of E. coli or S. aureus with 1 × 107 CFU/mL were inoculated into 800 μL of broth medium, and then 100 μL of samples with different concentrations (0, 75, 150, 300, 600, 1200 mg/L) were added. Furthermore, two groups of mixed bacterial liquids were prepared for the two kinds of bacteria, one group with laser, and the other without laser. After that, the mixed liquids were placed on a shaker and incubated for 18 h (37 °C, 140 rpm).

The morphology of bacterial was characterized by SEM. The bacteria after different treatments and the control group were cultured at 37 °C for 4 h according to the previous report [32]. Firstly, the bacteria were collected by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 3 min. Then, they were fixed with 35% glutaraldehyde in 30 min. After that, the bacterial were dehydrated with a series of different concentrations of ethanol (30–100%). Finally, the samples were dropped onto silicon wafers to dry, then sputtered with gold in a vacuum evaporator for 1 min and characterized by SEM.

To prepare the nanofiber membrane, we used the electrospinning method as previously reported [16]. Briefly, PVP was dissolved in ethanol to form a 15 wt% solution，and then, the 500 μL ESM/CuS solution and equal volume ethanol were added and the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 1 h to prepare a uniform dark green gelatinous solution with the CuS concentration of about 100 mg/L. Then, we replaced the ethanol in the above steps with an equal volume of CDs, and the ESM/CuS/CDs electrospinning solution was ready for use. After that, these electrospinning precursor solutions were poured into a syringe and fixed in the handheld electrospinning device. The spinning voltage is 10 kV, the diameter of the spinning nozzle is 0.4 mm, the distance between the spinning nozzle and the collector is about 10 cm. The nanofibers were collected on the aluminum foil or spun on the wound in situ.

The cell viability of electrospun nanofiber membranes on human fibroblasts (HS68) was measured by CCK-8 method [36]. Simply put, 5000 cells were dispersed in each well of the 96-well plate and pre-culture for 24 h in an incubator. The electrospun nanofiber membranes of different qualities (with the 30 μg/mL concentration of CDs) were mixed in 1 mL medium, then 100 μL was added to 96-well plate and incubate for 24 h. After that, old medium was aspirated out, 100 μL of new medium and 10 μL of CCK-8 solution were added, incubated for 60 min. Finally, the absorbance at 450 nm was monitored by a microplate reader.

The antioxidant capacity of CDs was studied by DPPH free radical scavenging assay to verify its ability to trap free radicals according to Wu's method [37]. Absolute ethanol was used to prepare DPPH solution with a concentration of 0.01 mM. First, 1.5 ml of ethanol and 0.5 ml of CDs were added into the centrifuge tube, then, 1 ml of DPPH was added, and they were incubated in the dark for 40 min at room temperature. Then, a microplate reader was used to record the absorbance of the mixed solution. In addition, the group without CDs as the control group was carried out under the same conditions. Finally, the percentage of DPPH removal effect was calculated according to the following formula:
DPPH Scavenging effect
%
=
1 −
A S
A C
× 100
Among them, As is the absorbance of the sample, and Ac is the absorbance of the control group.

The studies carried out in this work pertaining to full-thickness wound repair on animal modes using the above materials were ethically approved by the ethical committee of Quanzhou Normal University (No. QZTC-2021.0002). The in vivo animal experiments were adapted by Dhara's method [38]. A total of 30 male SD rats with an average weight of ca. 180 g were studied. To do the experiments, 6 weeks old SD rats were voluntarily anesthetized with 10% chloral hydrate, and then a circular area (about 7 mm in diameter) was cut on the back and both sides of the shoulder of each mouse with surgical scissors. Then 50 μL of MRSA bacterial suspension (1.75 × 107 CFU/mL, provided by the Second Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University) was used to infect skin tissues of rats. After 24 h, pus was observed on the infected wound. After that, these rats were divided into 6 groups: control group, NIR group, ESM/CuS group, ESM/CuS + NIR group, ESM/CuS/CDs group, ESM/CuS/CDs + NIR group. The wounds were treated differently, and the irradiation conditions were set as 980 nm NIR light for 5 min. We observe the recovery of the wound and obtain the skin samples for further analysis. The wounds changes were recorded at each time interval i.e., 2 d, 4 d, 6 d, 8 d, 10 d.

Moreover, the skin samples of the mice after 10 days surgical treatment were collected for H&E staining. The wound tissue was fixed on the glass slides and stained with hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) to evaluate the progress of wound recovery. Then, the histopathological changes of the wound were observed under a microscope.

The mouse tail amputation model was used to determine the hemostatic properties of electrospun fibers made of different materials. Firstly, 9 SD rats were anesthetized and randomly divided into ESM/CuS, ESM/CuS/CDs groups and a control group without any treatment. Then, 1/4 of the tail was cut off, and a manual electrospinning device was used to spin different materials on the wound for treatment. Finally, the hemostasis time and blood loss were calculated.

The UV–vis spectroscopy was obtained by a Spectrophotometer (METASH UV-9000S). And the morphology of the ESM/CuS nanocomposites and CDs were characterized by a Transmission electron microscope (TEM) (H-7650, Hitachi, Japan). The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of CDs was measured on a photoluminescent spectrophotometer (Techcomp FL970, Shanghai, China). The crystal structure of the CuS NPs was measured by a Bruker (D8 Advanced) X-ray diffractometer (XRD) with a Cu Kα radiation source (λ = 1.5418 Å) generated at 40 kV and 40 mA. The diffraction patterns were recorded in the 2θ range of 10–80° with a scan rate of 10° min−1. Zeta potentials and size of the CuS NPs were measured by zeta-sizer nano analyzer (Nano ZS90, Malvern, UK). Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) Spectroscopy was recorded on a Thermo Nicolet spectrometer in the range of 4000–400 cm−1. The morphologies of the bacterial and nanofibers were characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Merlin Compact, ZEISS, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and properties of the ESM/CuS NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis-NIR absorption of ESM/CuS NPs was recorded as shown in Fig. 1A, the ESM/CuS NPs exhibited a strong NIR adsorption ability at 1080 nm. To confirm the material has photothermal performance, different concentrations of ESM/CuS NPs and water (as control) were exposed to a 980 nm laser at the power density of 2.5 W/cm2 for 10 min. Fig. 1B exhibited the temperature changes of a series of different solutions with time. One can see that the higher concentration of ESM/CuS NPs can bring about the higher temperature changes at the same time, and the temperature rose slowly as time increased. Moreover, the temperature for water only changed from 25 to 39 °C in 10 min, while the solution of ESM/CuS NPs with the concentration of 600 mg/L increased from 25 to 56 °C at the same time. The results indicated that the ESM/CuS NPs had favorable photothermal performance and could effectively convert NIR laser irradiation into heat, proving a great potential to be used as an excellent photothermal agent for sterilization. Furthermore, the XRD pattern of as-prepared ESM/CuS NPs was also measured (Fig. 1C). The diffraction peaks of 2θ located at 29.54°, 32.11°, 39.13°, 48.17° corresponding to (102), (103), (105), (107) planes of covellite-phase CuS (JCPDS No. 75-2235), which proved the successful synthesis of CuS crystal structure. To further study the structural features of ESM/CuS NPs, we used FT-IR to characterize the functional groups on the surface of the NPs (Fig. 1D). The broad peak at around 3286.3 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH2 [39], and the peaks at 2924.9 and 2851.9 cm−1 were associated to the vibration of CH [40]. At 2426.2 cm−1, a peak attributed to the vibration of the sulfhydryl group (-SH) can be observed, which is due to the S-containing amino acid of proteins in the dissolved eggshell membrane [41]. It is worth mentioning that the inert SS bonds can be activated to react with Cu2+ and generate CuS NPs coated by proteins in alkaline solution, affording them biocompatible surfaces. Moreover, the peaks at 2361.9 and 2330.2 cm−1 could be attributed to the asymmetrical stretching vibration of CH bond from CH and CH2 groups [42].The vibration of CO groups can be observed at 1782.5 cm−1, which proved the existence of carboxyl groups on the surface of the ESM/CuS NPs [43]. In addition, two peaks at 1562.5 and 1384.9 cm−1 correspond to the symmetrical and asymmetrical vibrations of COO−, which was complexed with the surface Cu centers [44]. And the band at 834.9 cm−1 can be attributed to the out-of-plane bending vibration of CH, which may be due to the presence of aromatic hydrocarbons from proteins. In summary, the FT-IR spectrum of ESM/CuS NPs proved the existence of NH2, COOH, SH, etc. groups, which enhanced the water solubility of the material, and may also play a potential role in improving the cell functions.

TEM was further used to determine the morphological characteristics of ESM/CuS NPs. As shown in Fig. 2A, the freshly prepared ESM/CuS NPs were spherical with small particle size and good dispersibility. Moreover, it could be seen that the particle size was mainly distributed between 2.5 and 4.5 nm from the histogram of the size distribution (inset in Fig. 2A). Interestingly, when the freshly prepared solution was left for one day, it was found that the originally small-sized nanoparticles has grown up (Fig. 2B). And it has been transformed into spherical-like NPs with a size range of 50–60 nm (inset in Fig. 2B), which increased approximately 15 times compared with the original one. This inferred that the protein played a key role in directing the growth and assembly of ESM/CuS NPs. Also, the increase in size of nanoparticles may be due to the strong interaction (covalent bond, hydrogen bond and van der Waals force) between the protein template and the nanoparticles, or the interaction between amino acids, which led to the dynamic self-assembly process of nanoparticles [2,45,46]. Based on this, one can control the sizes of CuS NPs by adjusting the ratio of between Cu2+ ions and eggshell membrane proteins.

In addition, the (hydrodynamic) size and zeta potential of ESM/CuS NPs were also measured by a zeta-sizer nano analyzer (Nano ZS90, Malvern). As shown in Fig. S1A, the size of the NPs was about 235.5 nm, which was much larger than the one in TEM images. This is because that the functional groups on the surface of the particles (such as carboxyl groups, carbonyl groups, etc.) may cause them to adsorb together to form larger aggregates in the liquid [47–49]. In addition, since the ESM/CuS NPs are negatively-charged (Fig. S1B, zeta potential = −111 mV), the interaction between the biological macromolecules and ions will cause the coating of an adsorption layer on the surfaces of CuS NPs, affording them biocompatible interfaces for the following functions [22]. Although the coated-ESM is primarily composed of collagens (Types I, V, X; 10%) and other proteins (glycoproteins, keratin, osteopontin, histones, etc.) with complex components, it is a potential biomaterial for tissue engineering applications. Moreover, an in-depth analysis of ESM proteome was done by Hincke et al., who identified 472 proteins in ESM and some of them demonstrated antibacterial activities [50]. Meanwhile, Hincke et al. also reported that partialized ESM processed by cryo-grinding and homogenization exhibited antimicrobial activity against skin-associated pathogens [51]. Cryo-grinding is a technique where ESM is processed by cooling and liquid nitrogen, which maintain the structure and function of proteins to a great extent. Compared with these facts, herein, it is worth noting that the hydrolyzed ESM treated by NaOH (in our system) did not show any antibacterial activity for either E. coli. or S. aureus (Fig. S2), which might be attributed to the disruption of tertiary structure and function losing in proteins. To fully exert ESM advantages, a mild treating way of ESM should be actively explored soon.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal ablation against S. aureus and E. coli
ce:section.ce:para: The high photothermal performance of ESM/CuS NPs has promoted us to investigate their photothermal sterilization ability. Gram-negative bacteria E. coli and Gram-positive bacteria S. aureus were selected as representative strains in this experiment. As shown in Fig. 3A, NIR irradiation only had a small amount of sterilization effect in the case of no ESM/CuS NPs, either E. coli or S. aureus. However, after adding the ESM/CuS NPs without NIR irradiation, the number of colonies on the plate was greatly reduced, regardless of E. coli or S. aureus. This proved that the Cu-containing material has a certain bactericidal performance on both bacteria. Furthermore, with the addition of ESM/CuS NPs under the NIR irradiation, these two bacteria were almost completely killed. It is obvious that there are no surviving colonies on the plate, so the sterilization effect was much stronger than that only adding CuS NPs group. Furthermore, the bactericidal effects of CuS NPs and NIR can be seen more intuitively from the statistical histogram in Fig. 3B and C. The control group has a lower bactericidal effect under NIR irradiation (36% for E. coli and 41% for S. aureus). As a comparison, the antibacterial efficiency of only adding CuS NPs to the bacterial solution was sharply increased (70% for E. coli and 81% for S. aureus). However, the combination of CuS NPs and NIR can achieve almost 100% sterilization rate for both bacteria. Therefore, ESM/CuS NPs were confirmed that they could have an excellent photothermal sterilization performance under the irradiation of NIR.

To further evaluate the antibacterial performance of the as-prepared material, different concentrations of ESM/CuS NPs were co-cultured with bacteria. The right side of Fig. S3 showed the turbidity changes of different concentrations of ESM/CuS NPs (from right to left: 0, 75, 150, 300, 600, 1200 mg/L) after incubation with E. coli and S. aureus for 18 h (with or without NIR irradiation). For E. coli (Fig. S3A), when the concentration was 300 mg/L, the bacterial suspension incubated with ESM/CuS NPs without NIR irradiation was turbid, which indicated that the bacteria could survive well. As a contrast, the NIR-irradiated samples had become clear at this concentration, proving that most bacteria had lost their biological activities. Therefore, it can be estimated that the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of ESM/CuS NPs was 300 mg/L under NIR radiation. A similar phenomenon was also observed in the treatment of S. aureus (Fig. S3B), except that the MIC was 150 mg/L under NIR irradiation. Obviously, NIR radiation was essential for the survival of E. coli and S. aureus in the presence of ESM/CuS NPs. Furthermore, the cultured bacterial liquid was cultured on a solid medium, and the result was shown on the left side of Fig. S3. It could be seen that the minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) of the ESM/CuS NPs on E. Coli was 1200 mg/L under no NIR irradiation, but after NIR irradiation, the MBC was reduced to 600 mg/L (Fig. S3A). As for S. aureus (Fig. S3B), without NIR, there was no growth of bacteria on the plate when the concentration was 600 mg/L, while the same phenomenon can be observed at 300 mg/L after using NIR. This proved that the antibacterial performance of the material against S. aureus was slightly better than that of E. coli, and the use of NIR could significantly reduce the MBC.

Fig. 4 was the calculated antibacterial efficiency by counting the number of colonies in Fig. S3. It can be seen intuitively from the figures that the antibacterial effect gradually increased as the material concentration increased. And at each concentration, the antibacterial efficiency of NIR irradiation was higher than that without NIR. Fig. 4A showed the antibacterial performance of ESM/CuS NPs on E. coli. When the concentration was 600 mg/L, the sterilization rate with NIR was almost 100%, while that one without laser was only 55%. For S. aureus (Fig. 4B), the similar phenomena can be observed at a concentration of 300 mg/L (95% for NIR and 86% for without NIR). The above results further showed that the eggshell membrane-templated material had an excellent photothermal effect and could be used for sterilization.

Moreover, to further explore the reasons for sterilization (as mentioned above, the influence of ESM itself was ruled out.), we used SEM to observe the morphology of bacteria under different treatment conditions. Fig. 5A–D showed the external morphology of E. coli. Firstly, the morphology of bacteria cultured without nanomaterials was observed (Fig. 5A), it was found that the cell surface was slightly wrinkled due to dehydration treatment, but the morphology was regular and the structure remained intact. After NIR irradiation (Fig. 5B), one could find that the bacteria were slightly deformed, but the morphology was basically same as that observed without light. When they were co-cultivated with ESM/CuS NPs (Fig. 5C), lots of nanoparticles were found around the E. coli, and the bacterial were significantly smaller than the control group. This may be because that the material adhered to the surface of the bacteria and interacted with the cell wall, influencing the permeability of the cell wall and leading to the further shrinkage of the bacteria. With the addition of ESM/CuS NPs and NIR irradiation (Fig. 5D), bacteria were found deformed and broken in various degree. And some bacterial had small pits, indicating that the intracellular content had been leaked out. For S. aureus, as shown in Fig. 5E, the surface of the bacteria in the control group was smooth and intact, showing a spherical shape, and there was no significant morphology change in the only NIR-irradiation group (Fig. 5F). When co-cultured with the material (Fig. 5G), a phenomenon similar with E. coli was observed. The surfaces of bacteria were covered by a large amount of ESM/CuS NPs. After NIR irradiation (Fig. 5H), not only a great number of substances were found adhering to the surface of the bacteria, but a small number of cells were also observed to deform and appear holes, which would cause cytoplasm to overflow and lead to cell apoptosis. Combining the two types of bacteria, it can be speculated that the enhanced antibacterial performance of laser irradiation may be related to local high temperature ablation. This indicated that NIR irradiation could effectively help ESM/CuS NPs enter the bacteria through the holes formed by ablation and lead to the death of bacteria by aptosis.

In summary, the reason for the sterilization of ESM/CuS NPs may be due to the strong interaction between the nanocomposites and the bacterial. According to the report made by Zardini et al., multi-walled carbon nanotubes functionalized with amino acids can enhance the antibacterial property [52]. Some functional groups or little peptides remained on the surface of ESM/CuS NPs may interact effectively with the cell wall, affect bacterial viability, and destroy its structure. However, it was not a major reason because the soluble ESM exhibited negligible antibacterial activity as mentioned before. On the other hand, copper ions may be released from CuS NPs, which change the permeability of the bacterial cell wall, prevent the cells from absorbing nutrients, and ultimately affect the growth and vitality of the cells, thereby effectively causing the inactivation of the bacteria. It is worth noting that considering the complex influencing factors caused by nanoparticle explosion, bubble formation and thermal decomposition, the detail molecular mechanism is still unclear, and further exploration is needed in the future [32,53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs and preparation of composite nanofibers
ce:section.ce:para: As a new class of carbon-based nanomaterials, CDs have the characteristics of abundant surface functional groups, high chemical stability, good biocompatibility, and easy interaction with substrates, have been extensively explored in many other biological fields (labeling, sensing, drug delivery, and antibacterial). However, the wound healing effect by adding CDs is rarely reported. Herein, we combined the chitosan-derived CDs with ESM/CuS NPs as well as PVP to achieve an ideal effect of promoting wound healing. As shown in Fig. 6A, the maximum excitation and emission spectra of the as-prepared CDs were recorded as 340 nm and 420 nm, respectively. To explore whether the synthesized CDs exhibited an excitation-dependent emission behavior or not, we used different excitation wavelengths to identify it. As shown in Fig. 6B, the fluorescence intensity with an excitation wavelength of 320 nm was less than that of 340 nm, but as the excitation wavelength increased (340–460 nm), the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased, and showed the highest value at 340 nm. Moreover, to observe the morphology and size characteristics of the synthesized CDs particles, we used HR-TEM to further identify them. It can be seen from Fig. 6C that the CDs had a particularly small particle size, which were nearly circular and had better dispersibility. From the histogram of the inset, one could see that most of the particles were distributed in the range of 1.5–2.5 nm. In addition, the lattice fringe spacing (0.18 nm) measured in Fig. 6D corresponded to the (102) plane of graphitic carbon.

As well known, the wound will produce a large amount of oxygen free radicals under the environmental, physical, and chemical effects, which will increase the permeability of the capillary wall and damage the vascular endothelial cells, and aggravate the edema and hypoxia of the injured area and adjacent tissues, resulting in more extensive secondary damages such as cell necrosis. Therefore, radicals scavenging ability is of paramount importance during the process of wound repairing. Due to the existence of various functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl on the surface of CDs, it has been reported that the CDs has the free radicals-scanning ability. Thus, we attempted to study the he antioxidant capacity of as-prepared CDs by using DPPH clearance experiments. As shown in Fig. S4, compared with the control group, CDs showed a 50.2% of scavenging effect on DPPH, which proved that it had a high free radical scavenging ability and laid a foundation for its role on wound healing. Furthermore, the cell viability of the CDs was also measured. As shown in Fig. S5, there was no obvious reduction in cell viability even at a high CDs concentration (30 μg/mL), revealing the good biosafety.

Next, we used a hand-held electrospinning instrument to prepare the composite nanofibers by combining the mixed solution of ESM/CuS NPs, CDs, as well as PVP. As shown in Fig. 7, the used hand-held device is about 15 cm in length and 5 cm in width. When the electrospinning solution is filled in the syringe, one presses the switch and the device start working. It can be seen from the movie (Supporting information) that this instrument can quickly electrospun a layer of film (~10 s) on the surface of hand, and the film will become thick at 60 s. When the distance between the device and the back of the hand is about 6 cm, a circle membrane with a diameter of ca. 4 cm is formed, which can be easily torn off. Compared with traditional electrospinning instrument, the hand-held device is convenient to be used for on-site emergency.

Next, we used a hand-held electrospinning instrument to prepare the composite nanofibers by combining the mixed solution of ESM/CuS NPs, CDs, as well as PVP. As shown in Fig. 7, the used hand-held device is about 15 cm in length and 5 cm in width. When the electrospinning solution is filled in the syringe, one presses the switch and the device start working. It can be seen from the movie (Supporting information) that this instrument can quickly electrospun a layer of film (~10 s) on the surface of hand, and the film will become thick at 60 s. When the distance between the device and the back of the hand is about 6 cm, a circle membrane with a diameter of ca. 4 cm is formed, which can be easily torn off. Compared with traditional electrospinning instrument, the hand-held device is convenient to be used for on-site emergency.

Fig. 7B and C showed the pictures of electrospun nanofibers with different materials. Obviously, the appearance of both membranes was white, indicating the neglected influence upon the addition of CuS NPs and CDs. However, under ultraviolet light, the appearance was totally different. The membrane made up of ESM/CuS (Fig. 7B) without addition of CDs showed bright lavender and no fluorescent CDs characteristics; in contrast, The membrane made up of ESM/CuS/CDs (Fig. 7C) with addition of CDs exhibited characteristic blue fluorescence. It is worth noting that the blue color is homogeneously distributed inside the membrane, revealing the good compatibility with the matrix.

Furthermore, in order to prove the successful preparation of composite nanofibers, FT-IR was carried out. It can be seen from Fig. 8 that both PVP and PVP/ESM/CuS membranes exhibited strong peaks at 1651.4 and 1287.2 cm−1, corresponding to CO and CN stretching vibration, respectively, indicating that the matrix of nanofibers was PVP [16]. Moreover, the peaks at 2361.9 and 2330.2 cm−1 corresponded to CH bond from CH and CH2, which were consistent with the peaks of ESM/CuS NPs in Fig. 1D. In addition, the CO vibration originally appeared in 1782.5 cm−1 shifted slightly to 1741.8 cm−1, which may be due to the coordination between PVP and the composites. The above three peaks only appeared in the composite fibers, proving that the PVP was doped with ESM/CuS NPs.

The main purpose of this experiment is to reproduce the structure of the ESM by electrospinning, so that the nanofibers not only have structural advantages, but also has functional properties such as antibacterial and good biocompatibility to promote the wound healing. In order to compare the micro morphology of electrospinning and natural ESM, SEM characterization was performed. As shown in Fig. 9A, B and C, the pictures displayed the nanofibers prepared by the hand-held electrospinning device. The images showed that the composite nanofibers have regular morphology and narrow distribution, with a layered porous network structure. It is worth mentioning that the electrospinning diameter of pure PVP was about 900 nm (Fig. 9A), which was larger than the nanofibers prepared by adding ESM/CuS (Fig. 9B, diameter is about 400–500 nm) and CDs-ESM/CuS (Fig. 9C, diameter is about 400–600 nm). This may be due to the addition of ESM/CuS NPs in the PVP solution, and the conductive liquid can carry more charges during the electrospinning process, which lead to an increase in the conductivity of the solution, so made the fiber thinner. Fig. 9D was a SEM image of a natural ESM with a 3D layered network structure, which was made up of fibers with a diameter between 0.7 and 1.5 μm, similar with the structure of a spun fiber membrane. It is reported that the unique 3D network structure of the ESM can promote the oxygen exchange of the wound [16], and it contains certain amino acids and other components that can resist pathogens, thus facilitating wound healing and serving as wound dressings. In summary, ESM-mimicking multifunctional nanofibers can effectively promote skin wound healing.

Cell biocompatibility is a critical indicator of whether it can be used in biomedicine, so we evaluated the cytotoxicity of composite membrane. The prepared ESM/CuS/CDs membrane was cut into different qualities (0.5, 1, 3, 5 mg), and then, human fibroblast was used to incubated with the fiber membrane for 24 h. As shown in Fig. S4, when the quality of composite membrane was 0.5 mg, the cells maintain the high viability of 91.3%. And when the mass reached as high as 2 mg, it still showed an acceptable activity (85.1%), indicating that the cytotoxicity of ESM/CuS/CDs membrane was low and had the potential to be applied in biology.

The first step in the treatment of bleeding wounds is to stop bleeding, stimulate the accumulation of platelets in the injured tissue and form blood clots to prevent massive bleeding. This operation also affects the subsequent treatment steps, so the hemostatic function of the composite nanofiber membrane is an important point that we cannot ignore. As shown in Fig. 10 , the hemostatic ability of the composite fiber membrane was evaluated by measuring the amount of blood loss and the hemostatic time in the mouse tail docking experiment. It can be seen directly from Fig. 10A, B and C that compared with the control group, the blood loss of the ESM/CuS group was only slightly reduced, while the gauze of the CDs-ESM/CuS group was significantly different, the blood volume on the surface was much less than that of the other two groups. The hemostatic time and blood loss statistics from Fig. 10D and E also verified the observation results. Compared with the time required for hemostasis in the control group (~5.7 min) and ESM/CuS group (~5.1 min), the ESM/CuS/CDs group reduced the hemostasis time to 4.1 min, which greatly shortened the hemostasis time and further accelerated wound healing. In addition, compared with the control group, the blood loss of the ESM/CuS group did not change significantly, but only decreased by 5.7%, which may be due to the covering effect of the nanofibrous membrane on the wound. The fiber membrane with CDs reduced the amount of bleeding by 69.4%, verifying the excellent hemostatic performance of this nanocomposite fiber membrane.

Based on the significant photothermal performance of the composite and low in vivo toxicity of composite membrane demonstrated above, we further explored the in vivo photothermal antibacterial ability. The results of animal epidermis experiment were shown in Fig. 11 . In the beginning (Day 0), the wounds infected by bacteria in each group showed abscesses. After 4 days, except for the wounds in the ESM/CuS/CDs and ESM/CuS/CDs + NIR groups, which showed a tendency to heal, there were no significant changes in the wounds of the other groups. With the increase of treatment time, the difference in wound healing of each group began to become obvious. After 8 days later, the wound area of the (ESM/CuS and ESM/CuS/CDs) + NIR groups were significantly smaller than that of the other groups, and the scab had already been formed. Post 10 days of incubation, compared with other groups, ESM/CuS/CDs + NIR group showed best effect in reducing wound size and abscesses. The minimum wound in this group was observed, which appeared slightly pink and almost healed. The rapid wound closure rat can be attributed to the synergistic effect of the photothermal sterilization of ESM/CuS, the anti-oxidation feature of CDs [54], as well as the adequate moist of electrospun nanofibers, which together promote wound healing.

Furthermore, the healing process was evaluated by histological examination. As shown in Fig. 12 , the H&E staining results of skin slices showed that lots of inflammatory cells in the control, single NIR, ESM/CuS nanocomposites, as well as ESM/CuS + NIR group groups, which also kept a big area of unmatured granulation tissue deposition around the wounds. In contrast, the ESM/CuS/CDs and ESM/CuS/CDs + NIR groups demonstrated the well-proliferated fibroblast, less inflammation of inflammatory cells, as well as less unmatured granulation tissue around the wound sites. The phenomenon could be assigned to the synergistic effects of eggshell membrane (to expedite cell proliferation) and CDs (to scavenge ROS). Following the initial hemostasis, shortening the inflammation phase of the ESM/CuS/CDs + NIR group is an important process for promoting wound healing.

In summary, it was shown that the thermal effect produced by the combination of ESM/CuS NPs and photothermal therapy, as well as the antioxidant effect of CDs, played an important role in enhancing the healing of infectious wounds.

In this study, the biocompatible ESM/CuS NPs and fluorescent CDs had been successfully prepared via the simple one-step methods. By using dissolved ESM as a soft template, the monodisperse ESM/CuS nanoparticles not only retained the certain bioactive components of natural biomaterials with good biocompatibility and stability, but also possessed the robust antibacterial properties. The NIR-irradiation experiments proved that the as-prepared nanomaterial had strong photothermal ability in 980 nm NIR wavelength irradiation and could be used for the excellent photothermal sterilization. Under NIR irradiation, the MBC of E. coli would be reduced from 1200 to 600 mg/L, and S. aureus from 600 to 300 mg/L. Meanwhile, by using chitosan as carbon sources, the blue-fluorescence CDs was prepared, which displayed the strong free radicals-scanning ability and hemostasis effects. Moreover, a hand-held electrospinning device was used to combine ESM/CuS NPs, CDs and PVP solution to form nanofiber, simulating the layered porous structure of ESM. This in-situ prepared nanofiber not only has ECM-like structural advantages, promoting gas exchange, but also possesses multiple functions of hemostasis and promoting wound healing. More importantly, this hand-held device can implement emergency medical aid in situ to fully display the functions of mixed solution. This work provided new insights into the value-added application of biological membranes (for example, eggshell membrane), CDs, as well as photothermal agents (such as, CuS) in promoting would healing [55–56].
